exemplar.title:
How can Markov Chain models be effectively utilized to analyze and predict the 
probabilistic transitions between different states in political conflicts, and what 
factors influence the stability and resolution of these conflicts?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear title and rationale that link directly to maximising Instagram engagement
Logical and accessible explanation of the differential-equation approach
Effective signposting with section headings that guide the inquiry
weaknesses: Research question is implied in the aim rather than stated explicitly
Limited justification for choosing a differential-equation model over simpler alternatives
Methodology relies on a narrow dataset and speculative parameter selection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The topic, purpose and focus are communicated accurately and fluently, consistent with the top band, but the research question is not formally stated and the methodology, while appropriate, shows limited evidence of informed source selection. These minor weaknesses justify a 5/6 rather than full marks.

strengths: Solid application of integrating factors, integration by parts and regression in context
Generally accurate use of subject-specific terminology (e.g. âdecay constantâ, âlocal maximumâ)
Calculations are mostly correct and applied directly to the Instagram data
weaknesses: Notation errors (P(x)=Î»E, inconsistent use of t vs x) and typos in the Python appendix
Numerical precision lost by rounding key parameters
Some definitions (e.g. period T) lack clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates good subject knowledge and mostly accurate terminology, aligning with the 3â4 band. However, recurring notational slips, imprecise numerical reporting and minor conceptual mis-statements prevent attainment of the top band, warranting a score of 4/6.

strengths: Relevant primary data collection linked to the research question
Comprehensive analytical solution of the ODE and parameter fitting
Logical derivation of conclusions and useful comparison with an empirical model
weaknesses: Short data-collection period and lack of secondary sources limit research depth
Evaluation remains largely qualitative without statistical goodness-of-fit metrics
Assumptions (negative engagement, constant Î») are acknowledged but not rigorously addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality is good but not consistently excellent (band 4â6), analysis depth is strong (band 7â9) and evaluation is only partially critical (band 4â6). Taken holistically, the studentâs critical-thinking performance sits solidly in the lower half of the 7â9 band, justifying 7/12.

strengths: Logical overall structure with clear introduction, modelling, validation and conclusion sections
Section headings, numbered tables and figures support reader comprehension
weaknesses: Missing word count and inconsistent page numbering
LaTeX notation occasionally renders poorly and code fragments disrupt page flow
Graphs lack axis labels and units; table formatting is inconsistent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the reportâs structure fully supports understanding (band 3â4), several layout and formatting requirements are unmet (band 1â2). These mixed strengths and weaknesses align with a mid-band performance, meriting 3/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A focused question is evident â essentially âwhat posting time maximises engagement on the schoolâs Instagram account?â â and it drives the subsequent modelling and data collection.  However, the wording is embedded inside the aim instead of being stated formally, and some later discussion (e.g. negative values of engagement) shows that the boundaries of the inquiry were not fully refined.  Hence the strand meets the descriptors for a clear but only partially focused research question.

The mathematical analysis is extensive: a full analytical solution of the ODE, parameter fitting, derivative analysis and comparison with an empirical cubic model.  Conclusions (optimal time â 18.3 h) are logically derived and explicitly linked to the graphs.  Some assumptions (negative engagement, constant Î») are recognised but not rigorously resolved, so the analysis, while strong, is at the lower edge of the 7â9 band rather than approaching 10â12.

Mathematical terminology (e.g. âintegrating factorâ, âdecay constantâ, âcubic regressionâ, âlocal maximumâ) is used accurately and generally consistently throughout the essay.  A handful of syntax errors in the Python appendix and occasional typographical issues appear, yet these do not obscure meaning.  Overall usage aligns with the âmostly accurateâ descriptor of the 3â4 band.

Basic layout conventions (title page, contents table, embedded graphs) are met, yet page numbers are missing/inconsistent and LaTeX notation occasionally renders poorly.  The word count is absent and some table formatting is faulty.  Consequently, not all layout considerations are met, placing the work in the 1â2 band with the higher score for generally acceptable readability.

The student demonstrates solid knowledge of integrating factors, integration by parts, sinusoidal modelling and basic regression.  Most calculations are correct and are applied directly to the Instagram context.  Mis-statements such as writing P(x)=Î»E and a few notational slips prevent the work from reaching the excellence descriptor, but the application remains mostly relevant and shows good understanding.

The student begins with a clear title, a concise rationale, and an articulated aim that links directly to maximising Instagram engagement.  The explanation of why a differential-equation model is appropriate is logical and accessible to a non-expert reader, and the section headings signpost the inquiry effectively.  Minor weaknesses remain (e.g. the research question is implied rather than explicitly phrased), but overall the topic, purpose and focus are communicated accurately and fluently, consistent with the top band.

The essay follows a logical structure (introduction, modelling, numerical solution, validation, conclusion, appendix).  Section headings, numbered tables and figures support comprehension, and mathematics is placed where it is discussed.  Minor issuesâsuch as code fragments breaking page flowâare present but do not impede understanding, satisfying the top band for structure.

The student gathers primary data covering 13 time points and links it to the research question through parameter estimation and a regression check.  The data are relevant and generally sufficient to support the analysis, though the sample period is short and no secondary sources on social-media behaviour are consulted.  Thus research quality is good but not consistently excellent.

A reasoned argument is presented that evaluates the strengths and limitations of the model (e.g. negative values, idealised decay, parameter choice).  The comparison with the cubic regression is helpful, yet the evaluation remains largely qualitative and does not explore alternative models or statistical error measures.  This aligns with a partially critical evaluation characteristic of the 4â6 band.

The methodology combines (i) solving a first-order linear differential equation, (ii) collecting one month of engagement data and (iii) choosing parameters by inspection.  While these steps are appropriate and mostly explained, the range of sources is narrow (only the studentâs own dataset) and the choice of Î» is acknowledged as speculative rather than data-driven.  Evidence of informed selection is therefore present but limited, placing the work in the middle band.

annotations.comment: The absence of a word count at the start indicates the work does not meet IB formatting requirements. Please include a clear word count at the beginning.
No page number appears on this pageâs footer. Consistent pagination should be applied to every page.
The aim embeds the research question without a clear formal statement. An explicit question such as âWhat posting time maximizes engagement?â would sharpen focus.
The table of contents cell widths vary, causing misalignment. Standardise column widths and cell padding for improved readability.
The definition of baseline engagement \(B\) uses an inline bullet; formatting is inconsistent with earlier list styles. Use matching bullets or numbering.
The arrow notation in the integrating factor derivation ("â e^{\lambda t}") is ambiguous. Replace arrows with equality signs and clarify each step.
The rationale vividly describes motivation but lacks critical justification for choosing a differential-equation approach. Briefly explain why alternatives (e.g. regression alone) were insufficient.
Finiteâdifference notation $$\frac{ÎE}{Ît}$$ is used instead of the standard derivative notation $$\frac{dE}{dt}$$. For clarity and rigour, use $$dE/dt$$.
The list item for \(T\) uses a bullet but does not define the period in hours. Specify â\(T\): duration of one engagement cycle in hoursâ to avoid ambiguity.
The narrative mentions âchanges and clarificationsâ in deriving the general solution but does not list them clearly. Consider bullet-pointing each algebraic transformation.
In the standard form $$\frac{dy}{dx}+P(x)y=Q(x)$$, writing \(P(x)=\lambda E\) is incorrect. It should be \(P(t)=\lambda\) since \(P\) multiplies \(y\), not \(E\) again.
Switching between \(Q(x)\) and \(Q(t)\) creates confusion. Keep the independent variable consistent (use \(t\)) throughout.
In the second integration by parts, \(du\) is derived as \(-\tfrac{2\pi t}{T}\sin(...)\,dt\) but should be \(-\tfrac{2\pi}{T}\sin(...)\,dt\) without the extra \(t\).
The expression for \(J\) in the first line omits the negative sign correctly when distributing constants. Re-check the coefficient of the integral term.
The cubic regression function uses \(x\) rather than the time variable \(t\). Consistent variable naming improves clarity.
The final solution includes +C without describing how this constant is determined from initial conditions. Use \(E(0)=5\) to solve for \(C\).
Time entries in the data table use bold and overline formatting (e.g. \(\overline{2}\)) inconsistently. Use standard numeric labels for each time point.
The decay constant \(\lambda=0.1\) is chosen arbitrarily. Consider fitting an exponential decay model to timestamped engagement data or citing literature for justification.
Setting the constant \(C=0\) ignores the initial engagement \(E(0)=5\). Solve for \(C\) using the condition at posting time.
The function \(f(x)=\lambda\sin x - \tfrac{2\pi}{T}\cos x\) is maximized but the corresponding time \(t\) is not derived. Calculate the phase shift to find the exact peak time.
The calculation for peak engagement includes multiple steps in one block. Break out each algebraic manipulation for clarity.
Rounding \(A=42.23\) to 42 in the plotted function reduces numerical precision. Use the full calculated value to maintain accuracy.
The graph lacks labeled axes and units. Include clear axis titles (e.g. âPosting Time (h)â and âEngagementâ) to improve interpretability.
Negative engagement values appear in the earlyâmorning interval. Propose a model modification (e.g. floor at zero) to enforce non-negativity.
The identification of \(t=5.6\) as a local minimum is based on the graph alone. Include an analytic or numerical root-finding argument for confirmation.
No goodnessâofâfit metrics (e.g. RÂ², residuals) accompany the cubic regression. Provide statistical measures to justify model selection.
The comparison remarks on âclose alignmentâ of theoretical and empirical curves are qualitative. Add RMSE or percentage error to quantify the fit.
The conclusion summarises results but never restates the explicit research question. Reaffirm the question and show how your answer addresses it.
Future extension proposes interacting differential equations but does not link to data limitations identified earlier. Tie extensions to specific gaps in your dataset.
